message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 24
Recent Green phase: No code change — the last few tests (197.5 rounding, and the two integration examples 165 & 160) were confirming tests for the already-complete quote-premium logic. All quote-side rules are now implemented and tested.

IMPORTANT do NOT write experiment-done.txt — 20 todo tests remain (claim processing, error handling, CLI entry point at src/cli.ts are NOT yet implemented).

Refactor while keeping all tests green. The quote logic is mature now — take a holistic look: is the module well-organized, are there any lingering smells, duplicated patterns, or naming issues across the whole file before we start adding the separate claim-processing subsystem? Apply improvements only if metrics improve. Measure APP mass / McCabe / cognitive / ESLint before and after.

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by measuring the current state of the implementation before deciding on any refactoring.
Now let me check the ESLint setup and run the measurements.

content.text:
PRE measurements:
- **ESLint smells: 0** (completely clean — no cognitive complexity, no duplicate strings, no magic numbers, no max-lines violations)
- **Tests: 24 passing, 20 todo** ✅

Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for each function.

## PRE Measurements: APP Mass + McCabe per function

**`componentBasePremium`** (line 42-43): `count === BLOCK_SIZE ? BLOCK_PREMIUM : count * COMPONENT_PREMIUM`
- McCabe: 1 + ternary = **2**
- Cognitive: 1
- Mass: bindings(count, BLOCK_SIZE, BLOCK_PREMIUM, count, COMPONENT_PREMIUM)=5, invocation(*)=2, conditional(?:)=4 → **11**

**`isComponent`** (45): McCabe **1**, mass: binding(item)1 + invocation(has)2 = ~3

**`isMainItem`** (47): McCabe **1** (the `!` isn't a branch), mass ~3

**`countByType`** (49-53): reduce loop + `??`
- McCabe: 1 + loop(reduce) + `??`(1) = **3**
- Cognitive: ~2
- Mass: loop(5) + invocations(reduce, set, get) + bindings → ~20

**`componentTotal`** (55-59): filter + reduce (2 loops) + invocation
- McCabe: 1 + 2 loops = **3**, Cognitive ~2

**`basePremium`** (61-66): filter + reduce
- McCabe: 1 + 2 loops = **3**

**`surchargeFor`** (70-77): filter + reduce + `&&`
- McCabe: 1 + 2 loops + `&&`(1) = **4**, Cognitive ~3

**`discountWhen`** (90-91): ternary → McCabe **2**

**`quotePremium`** (95-104): no branches in body (all delegated)
- McCabe: **1**, Cognitive: 0/1

**`runScenario`** (106-112): map + `index > 0`
- McCabe: 1 + loop(map) + comparison = **2-3**

The implementation is **already exceptionally clean**. Worst McCabe is `surchargeFor` at 4 — well below any concern. Cognitive complexity threshold is 10; nothing approaches it. Let me look holistically for the things the prompt asked about: organization, duplicated patterns, naming issues, before the claim subsystem is added.

Let me examine a few candidate observations more carefully.

input.description: Check uses of mainItemBase and BASE_PREMIUM index

content.text:
I found one genuine, low-risk improvement opportunity. There is a small **conceptual duplication / inconsistency**: line 64 indexes `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` directly, while line 77 uses the named helper `mainItemBase(item)` for the identical knowledge ("the base premium of a main item"). The knowledge of "how to look up a main item's base premium" is represented in two places.

Let me evaluate this as a refactoring.

## Naming Evaluation (First Priority)

Walking every function name:
- `componentBasePremium`, `isComponent`, `isMainItem`, `countByType`, `componentTotal`, `basePremium`, `mainItemBase`, `surchargeFor`, `curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge`, `discountWhen`, `isLoyal`, `isCursed`, `isHighlyEnchanted`, `quotePremium`, `runScenario`

All names clearly reveal intent and match the test vocabulary (base premium, surcharge, loyalty, follow-up, block, component). No renames warranted.

## Refactoring Decision: DRY (Rule 3) — unify base-premium lookup

Line 64's inline `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` duplicates the knowledge captured by `mainItemBase`. Using the helper in both places removes the duplication. Let me measure this candidate.

**Target function `basePremium`** PRE:
- Line 64: `sum + BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` — bindings(sum, BASE_PREMIUM, item.type) ≈ 3
- After: `sum + mainItemBase(item)` — binding(sum)1 + invocation(mainItemBase)2 + binding(item)1 = 4

This change *raises* APP mass (adds an invocation cost of +2) while only removing a conceptual duplication that ESLint does not flag (the string `BASE_PREMIUM` appears but `no-duplicate-string` threshold is about string literals, not identifiers). Let me verify ESLint truly doesn't flag it and that mass would rise.

The DRY change would **increase APP mass** (invocation cost) for a duplication ESLint does not report. Per the discipline — *if a measurement got worse, don't keep it* — this is a losing trade on the numbers. The two-line lookup is also arguably clearer inline.

Let me check the one other candidate: whether `quotePremium` is over the line budget or could be split, and whether there's any structural reorganization that improves a metric.

input.command: awk 'NR>=95 && NR<=104' src/claim-office.ts | grep -c . ; echo "---lines in quotePremium body---"

content.text:
`quotePremium` is 10 lines, well under the 30-line limit. Cognitive complexity 0. No structural split would improve any metric — it would only add invocation mass.

Let me do a final confirmation that there is genuinely no metric-improving change by checking each ESLint rule category is already satisfied (it is — 0 violations) and reconfirm the worst-case McCabe.

## Refactoring Evaluation Summary

I evaluated every candidate against the measurement discipline. Here are the PRE measurements:

**ESLint smells: 0** across the whole file (cognitive-complexity, max-depth, max-lines-per-function, max-params, no-duplicate-string, no-magic-numbers, no-collapsible-if, etc. — all clean).

**Worst-case functions by McCabe:**
| Function | McCabe | Cognitive | APP Mass | Naming |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `surchargeFor` | 4 | ~3 | ~28 | clear |
| `countByType` | 3 | ~2 | ~20 | clear |
| `componentTotal` | 3 | ~2 | ~24 | clear |
| `basePremium` | 3 | ~2 | ~22 | clear |
| `componentBasePremium` | 2 | 1 | 11 | clear |
| `quotePremium` | 1 | 0 | ~30 | clear |
| `runScenario` | 2 | 1 | ~16 | clear |

Worst McCabe is **`surchargeFor` at 4** — far below the cognitive threshold of 10, and it is McCabe-4 only because it correctly composes two filters with a guard. Flattening it (e.g., splitting the `&&`) would not reduce decision count, only relocate it.

**Refactoring candidates considered and rejected on measurement:**

1. **DRY: replace inline `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` (line 64) with `mainItemBase(item)`** — removes a conceptual duplication, but ESLint does not flag it (it's an identifier index, not a duplicated string literal), and it **raises** `basePremium`'s APP mass by +2 (adds an invocation). Losing trade on the numbers; the inline lookup is equally clear. **Rejected.**

2. **Extract sub-expressions from `quotePremium`** — body is 10/30 lines, cognitive 0, McCabe 1. Any split only adds invocation mass with no decision-count reduction. **Rejected.**

3. **Naming pass** — all 16 function names already reveal intent and match the spec's vocabulary (base premium, surcharge, loyalty, follow-up, block, component). **No renames.**

**No refactoring performed.** Every metric is at or near its floor for the behaviour expressed:
- ESLint smells: 0 (already clean)
- Max cognitive complexity: ~3 (threshold 10)
- Max McCabe: 4 (composition of two filters + one guard)
- APP mass: minimal — the only DRY change available *increases* mass
- Naming: all names reveal intent

The module is well-organized for the upcoming claim subsystem: constants grouped at top, pure helpers (`isComponent`/`isMainItem`/`isCursed`/`isHighlyEnchanted`) composable as predicates, and the generic `surchargeFor`/`discountWhen` combinators ready to be reused. Forcing a change here would trade clarity or mass for no measurable gain, violating the discipline.

**Tests: 24/24 passing** ✅ (no changes made, so green is preserved).

## Report

```
🔄 Refactoring Complete:
Refactoring: none possible — all metrics at/near floor, only available
             change (DRY base-premium lookup) increases APP mass
Smell delta: 0 → 0 (ESLint already clean)
Cognitive delta (worst, surchargeFor): 3 → 3 (unchanged, no change made)
APP mass delta: n/a (no change applied)
McCabe delta (worst, surchargeFor): 4 → 4 (unchanged)
Tests: All passing ✅ (24/24, 20 todo)
```

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_01-30-14_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` — left unchanged; quote logic is mature and clean, ready for the claim-processing subsystem.

I did not write `experiment-done.txt` — 20 todo tests (claim processing, error handling, CLI) remain unimplemented.
